content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Tell me a movie that left you staring at the wall for 20 minutes after it ended

**Score:** 72
**Body:** You know those films where the credits roll and you just sit there… blinking… questioning life, your choices, the universe, and whether you should go lie down?
Yeah. Those movies.


### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’m an old, and *The Deer Hunter* had *exactly* this impact on late-teenaged me, a few decades ago. I was stunned.
2. The Vanishing (1988)
3. Schindler’s List

Life is Beautiful
4. The sixth sense. That movie stuck with me for days.
5. Dear Zachary. After several months of seeing it rec'd on reddit, vaguely remembering the case in the news,  and reading the Wikipedia summary of it before watching it, I was confident I could get through it without the devastation everyone said it brings. 

I was so very wrong.
6. Cronenberg’s *Crash*
7. The Mist (2007)
8. My buddies and I saw Eternal Sunshine when it came out back in the day. All of us walked out of the theater, got in the car, and drove home without saying a word
9. Requiem for a Dream (2000)
10. BLUE VELVET. My friend and I saw it on opening day, knowing little about it except that it was David Lynch’s new movie. We staggered out of the theatre into harsh sunlight, barely able to speak.

## 2. Movies that hit different when you rewatch them as an adult

**Score:** 50
**Body:** Last night I rewatched La La Land at 25, and it hit completely different from when I first saw it at 16.
It’s kinda wild how some movies grow up with you and start meaning something new every time you revisit them.

If you have any films that gave you a totally different interpretation as you got older, I’d love some recommendations.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. All of them.
  - No man ever steps in the same river twice, for it's not the same river and he's not the same man. - Heraclitus
2. Space jam as a kid. Watch it again as an adult. It was so bad.
3. A Goofy Movie. Started with relating to Max, now I’m relating to Goofy.
Step brothers is still a good movie, but I feel so bad for them the older I get. It feels like making fun of mentally disabled adult men with the way they behave. They are too incompetent to live on their own.
4. Fievel goes west
5. I love The Royal Tenenbaums. I’ve watched it so many times and just enjoy the quirk and the soundtrack, aesthetics, etc. 

My dad passed away in April after an extended hospitalization from COVID. I was diagnosed with breast cancer in May, surgery in July, additional treatments and complications following. My dad lived with me for 8 years, so clearing out his things and closing his estate, dealing with nursing home bs, and having COVID myself, 2025, has sucked. A lot. 

Anyhow, towards the end, Chas says to Royal “I’ve had a rough year, Dad” and Royal says, “I know Chassie, I know”, it just left me completely broken. I’ve never cried during it before but I was outright howling at this.
  - I'm so sorry you lost your dad. Covid is brutal. 
6. I was telling someone about this yesterday: "Airplane!", but only a little. When I was a kid I didn't understand why Captain Oveur was asking that kid that visited the cockpit such silly, random questions. Never asked why.

Now I know.
  - Similarly, The Naked Gun movies.   I thought they were funny when 
i watched them as a child but when I watched them as an adult I realized how many jokes there were that I had missed.
7. Top Gun. As a kid I thought Maverick was super cool and the Iceman was a jerk. As an adult I rewatched it and you can clearly see that Maverick was a danger to everyone around him.

## 3. What is a scary movie everyone has to watch at least once?

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Hi there!! I’ve always enjoyed movies about possessions, religious elements. supernatural entities, serial killers, crime etc. 

Some of my all time favorite movies are Midsommar, Hereditary, Immaculate and The Possession. 

Didn’t necessarily enjoy Long Legs (until I researched it) or The Woman In The Yard, I did enjoy Presence, Terrifier, The Strangers and I Know What You Did Last Summer.

Would love to watch more movies that are like that. I’ve seen many of the newer ones, I’m pretty quick to watch any horror film in theatres. If anyone has any suggestions that might suit my fancy I’d appreciate it!!

Edit: PLEASE DONT RECOMMEND MOVIES I NAMED HERE LOL. I feel like that’s a given.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Shining
2. A Nightmare on Elm Street is a must for everyone 

But along your interests I would say Poltergeist
  - The original Poltergeist
3. The Exorcist: I wouldn't recommend watching by yourself.
4. 1408 with John Cusack. 

If you haven’t heard about it before, do not read anything about it and just go into it blind. 

It scared the heck out of me.
5. The O.G., mandatory, must see, horror list is as follows:

"The Innocents" (1961)

"Psycho" (1960)

"The Birds" (1963)

"The Haunting" (1963)

"Night of the Living Dead" (1968)

"Rosemary's Baby" (1968)

"The Last House on the Left" (1972)

"The Exorcist" (1973) Original Theatrical Cut

"Jaws" (1975)

"Carrie" (1976)

"Invasion of the Body Snatchers" (1978)

"Halloween" (1978)

"Dawn of the Dead" (1978)

"Alien" (1979)

"The Shining" (1980)

"Friday the 13th" (1981)

"An American Werewolf in London" (1981)

"The Howling" (1981)

"Poltergeist" (1982)

"The Thing" (1982)

"Evil Dead" (1982)

"A Nightmare on Elm Street" (1984)

"The Fly" (1986)

"Evil Dead II" (1987)

"Hellraiser" (1987)

That's the "mandatory" list that gets you into the club. 

Let me know if you want the "V.I.P.", bottle service section, entry list.
  - I have to admit I've never actually watched Psycho. The ending has been pretty much completely spoiled, so I've never actually sat down to watch the whole thing. 

But I've seen all the others. 

About the only one I can think of that might be missing is The Omen (1976).
6. Psycho (1960)
7. The Witch, Rosemary’s Baby
8. IT (Tim Curry, but both are excellent)

The Shining

The Exorcist 

Those are 3 definitive supernatural horror classics that are actually good IMO. There's lots more great horror movies. But if I had to narrow my list down I'd say those 3 are classics though...

## 4. Can anyone please recommend a so bad it's good Christmas film?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I absolutely love Christmas films, and every year I try to add a few low budget, so bad they're good, corny Christmas films to my list. Some of my favorites are Jack Frost (Horror) Hot Frosty, and Father Christmas Is Back. Any recommendations are appreciated. Bonus points if there is a well known actor/actress playing a role they are completely phoning in.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Santa Claus vs the Martians
  - Without even looking at the synopsis, that sounds right up my street.
2. Red One from last year is corny mindless fun
  - I’m convinced it was written by AI.
3. Jingle all the way isn't the best movie, and it can be pretty corny. Plus it has a ton of well know actors. Look it up if you haven't and let me know what you think.
4. Violent Night
5. On peacock they have a pee-wee‘s Playhouse Christmas special, that is pure 80s.
6. Not a movie, but Hog Father is a pretty fun twist on Christmas.
7. Santa With Muscles - Hulk Hogan as Santa Claus.
8. Can’t believe I’m the first to say this. But it’s doesn’t get any cornier than Ernest Saves Christmas. Even still I love that movie. LOL

## 5. Movies that feel like one long Twilight Zone episode

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I recently watched The Stuff from 1985 and it reminded me of watching a Twilight Zone episode in full length movie form

Looking for more films that feel similar to this. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm sure there are lots, off the top of my head I'd say Vivarium but mainly because I wanna follow this post and see what others will say
2. "Rosemary's Baby" (1968)

"THX-1138" (1971)

"The Andromeda Strain" (1971)

"Westworld" (1973)

"The Tenant" (1976)

"Network" (1976)

"Erasherhead" (1977)

"Coma" (1978)

"The Brood" (1978)

"River's Edge" (1986)

"Blue Velvet" (1986)

"Lost Highway" (1997)

"Being John Malkovich" (1999)

"Mulholland Drive" (2001)

"Stay" (2005)

"Synechdoche, New York" (2009)

"Beyond the Black Rainbow" (2010)

"Annihilation" (2016)

""Under the Silver Lake" (2018)

"I'm Thinking of Ending Things" (2020)

"Men" (2022)
  - Rosemary's Baby definitely. How alien the characters other than Rosemary act and the gaslighting is very Twilight Zone. 

I disagree with Lynch movies and some of the other surreal movies they are too abstract, Twilight Zone is far more linear and straightforward.
3. Hi plains drifter
4. The Black Phone

The Truman Show

Ex Machina

A Ghost Story

Gattaca

Predestination

Get Out

The Lobster

Groundhog Day (and any time loop film)

The Fly

10 Cloverfield Lane

Upgrade

Contact

Dark City

The Man From Earth

The Guest

Oxygen

Somewhere In Time

Faults

Monolith
5. The One I Love (2014).
6. Carnival of Souls (1962) - one that actually has a Twilight Zone episode based on it


Twilight Zone: The Movie (1983)
7. Repo Man
8. These aren't really similar to "The Stuff", but they definitely have a "Twilight Zone" vibe to them imho :-

Mirage (1965)

Tormented (1960)

The Philadelphia Experiment (1984)

Old (2021)
9. Planet of the Apes (1968) [written by Rod Serling]

Soylent Green (1973)

Save the Green Planet! (2003) or Bugonia (2025)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What are some of the best revenge scenes in films?

**Score:** 1453
**Body:** In picture: The Girl With The Dragon Tattoo (2011). 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sicario. 

"Time to meet God."
  - It was nothing personal. 

……for me it was personal.
2. Carrie at the prom
3. The first “John Wick” movie, 

Sinking the ship in “the African Queen”

Mark Wahlberg in “the Departed”
  - That moment they start building John Wicks character like, “what the fuck have you done?! You took the only thing he had!! He’s literally going to kill every single one of us.” The anticipation of what I was in for was so real.
4. "Man On Fire", Rocket launcher from the balcony scene "Forgiveness is between them and God, it is my job to arrange the meeting."
  - “Creasy's art is death. He's about to paint his masterpiece.”
  - So many good ones in this movie. 

“Anyone that profited, anyone that bats their eyes at me. I’m gonna kill them”

“Sit right there Samuel and if you move I’ll snatch the life right out of you”

“Ok, it’s off to the next life for you. Guarantee, you won’t be lonely”

“Wish? I wish. You had. More time”

Ok now I’ve gotta go watch it again!
5. The Crow (1996), Eric Draven taping T-Bird to his car seat and driving it off a pier with a flare in T-Bird's lap, and then lighting the crow symbol on fire.
6. Le Comte de Monte Cristo

## 2. What's a role from an actor you don't like that you actually enjoyed them in?

**Score:** 1292
**Body:** For me it's Ben Affleck in Gone Girl. In general I find his acting to range from average and unremarkable to straight up bad, but he was actually pretty perfect in Gone Girl. His inconsistent and slightly awkward acting worked really well for his character imo. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It freaked me out how much Ben Affleck resembled Scott Peterson in Gone Girl.
  - Ben Affleck is who I pictured the whole time I was reading the book.  He was made for this role.
2. I generally don’t enjoy watching Jared Leto, especially at the moment, but my word, he was excellent in Requiem for a Dream.
  - Dallas Buyers Club too. I always say idc how people feel about that man when he’s given a good script and goes all in he’s a fantastic actor.
3. Mark Wahlberg in Boogie Nights.
  - Yup this one. He gets a pass for The Departed as well
4. Tim Allen in Galaxy Quest

Galaxy Quest is one of my favorite movies and has an amazing cast. Not a fan of Tim Allen, but he was perfect for that role and totally delivered.
  - I heard somewhere about Alan Rickman saying "oh my God, I think he just experienced acting" about Allen's performance.
5. Alec Baldwin in *The Hunt for Red October*
6. Mark Ruffalo, Zodiac.

## 3. Netflix slop

**Score:** 1274

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. And that movies cost $320 Million🥴
  - this is the only reason i watched it. i had to see what burning $320 million looked like.
  - Ugh I could have spent that money on so many other things. Like bills and Uber Eats.
2. I found it very average but my boys (10 & 7) really liked it. I’ve had to sit through it a few times now
  - It’s a very good point. Are we really the audience? When I was a kid I watched all kinds of crap which has terrible ratings. Now I’m an adult I expect to like every movie I watch even if I’m not the audience 😅
    - Same. I loved Flight of the Navigator as a kid. Hadn’t watched it for maybe 30 years and watched it recently. It’s not a strong movie. Takes a long time to get going and I thought the main kid was a bit of a brat. But, at the time, I adored it and wanted to be the navigator. Movies are meant to mean different things to different people and age groups, egocentric to assume otherwise.
  - The most mature stance a person can have when critiquing any media is acknowledging that they are not the target market.
3. I liked the movie for a one time watch
  - Yeah, I had no feelings when it ended. It wasn't egregiously bad, but I didn't care enough to form an opinion on it.

I barely remember what it was about now.
4. There was no way that cast could capture the tone of the source material.

## 4. Who is the worst actor in the existence?

**Score:** 1054
**Body:** Personally, i think is steven seagal

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dancer Michael Flatley. He wrote, directed, produced, funded and starred-in a spy movie called Blackbird. Blimey o’reilly, is all I’ll say
  - I like to count all the hats he wears in that movie.
2. He looks like he’s made of felt.
  - Or the fuzzy side of Velcro.
  - Roast ham wearing a disguise.
3. Seagal may not be a great actor, but have you heard his music?

https://youtu.be/vLe_BZ1mo3I?si=kP6Mn21qluJxc6gv

It's even worse!
  - *Him want de punani*   

I wish I hadn’t clicked
4. Steven Seagal? I disagree!

He convinced millions that he was some sort of amazing martial artist.

Now ***that*** takes some acting chops!
5. I liked Under Siege and Marked for Death. The problem is, he thinks he is those characters in real life.
6. Tommy Wiseau

## 5. Movies that felt neither satisfying nor unsatisfying to end.

**Score:** 823
**Body:** The image is from the movie "The Revenant".

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. No Country For Old Men’s last scene loves to avoid satisfaction and that is inherently satisfying

Maybe that is a bad example but I keep thinking about how mundane it is
  - That's kind of the point of the whole movie. Bad stuff happens, but life is still generally mundane and we all die in the end (the sherrif's dream). The bad guy even gets away. Kind of the opposite of the very end of Fargo, tone wise. The bad guys are arrested and it ends with the mundane-ness of a happy couple laying in bed watching TV, looking forward to the future and not talking about death. I love both endings equally.
    - > The bad guy even gets away.

I like how his final scene humanizes him, shows that he is indeed vulnerable and not a force of nature. Even that horrifying quality is mundanitized. The bad guy gets away, yeah, but he is just a bad guy... just a guy.
2. Lost in translation
3. Castaway
4. This falls into the category of films where it takes me days to decide what I felt about it. 

A couple  that come to mind are:

2001: A space Odyssey

The Graduate

And I really like these films.
5. I liked Revenant. I think it was tragic …. but I still liked that the Native daughter was found and returned. And Tom Hardy got dead 😵 as deserved. 

Also Beau is Afraid 

And Men (2022) both are so strange . But Ari Aster films - all have big endings. Good or poor - they are big!!!
6. True Grit (2010) always has me feeling like this with the epilogue.
  - Oh, wow. I love the epilogue.
7. Anora

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. My thoughts on Paterson (2016)

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I watched it tonight and I liked it alot so I had to write my thoughts on it.

Paterson (2016) is about a week in the life of a former US marine turned bus driver who lives with his wife in Paterson, New Jersey and shares his name with the town. Paterson (Adam Driver) is a very routine man. Everyday he wakes up at some time past 6, kisses his wife and has his cereal and is off to work. His evenings also follow a routine of walking the dog and going for a beer at the local pub. That is essentially the plot of this film and I get why many people dislike it for its slow, minimalist nature but I absolutely adore it.

"One must imagine Sisyphus happy" — A very famous but tad overused quote from Albert Camus is a good way to describe Paterson (2016). Camus posits that to be happy is a choice, an open act of  defiance against the meaningless, random universe. I think this movie sums that up quite well. Paterson is "Sisyphus" and life is "the boulder". His life isn't exactly easy but he chooses to be happy and to not complain. That itself is an act of defiance.

 Lets start, with his wife Laura who is perhaps the best, realest character in the film. She is a superficial scatter-minded artist who lives at home but she is also the only one who truly tries to  understand Paterson and loves him and (she is clearly very remorseful at what the dog does later). Now, Paterson could be like the colleague of his and complain about her bohemian lifestyle or about her expensive tastes but he does not. He chooses to see past her adhd-like tendencies and her superficial surface to the caring, loving wife underneath. In doing so, he creates personal meaning in their relationship.

Next, his work, in real life, being a Bus driver is definitely not easy. The hours are long, the pay is bad and those windows must not be good on the eyes or skin. But Paterson still does his job without complaint. He amuses himself as a fly on the wall during conversations between passengers and he smiles as he drives. He takes the quite moment between work during lunch or before he starts on his route to focus on his true passion which is poetry. In this way, he makes the task of rolling up the boulder everyday easier.

Speaking of his poetry: His reserved nature leads to him not being able to share his work with other like minded people. But that does not discourage him from continuing to write. The emphasis on William Carlos Williams' book of early works suggests that perhaps Paterson thinks that he hasn't yet his stride as a poet? But even so, the very act of drawing inspiration from the events of a mundane life and a marriage to create art is the greatest act of defiance against meaninglessness.

Paterson himself seems like someone that might be traumatized from the war. The focus on his marine picture, his calm reaction to the bus breaking down and his humored reaction to people thinking it might explode and his action when he thinks Everett might have an actual gun and his general quite nature suggest that he carries the psychological wounds of battle with him. But even, despite that to continue to be courteous and polite to everyone he meets is quite admirable.

So, every situation, even the crucial saddening tearing of the note book Paterson takes in stride and he makes the best, most tolerable outcome for himself in every situation, time and place. Or simply, he wakes up everyday and pushes the boulder up the mountain despite the banal, dreary and meaningless nature of life.

I think Adam Driver portrayed him well. Also what were the twins about?? I really like how people who actually pay attention can have so many interpretations of this seemingly boring, "nothing burger" film to the average cinema go-er. A funny one I read posited that the wife was dead all along. So it could be an excercise in absurdism or it could be about a dead wife for all I know?  

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Well said. I also really liked this film for the same reasons, but I can see why it’s not for everyone. It’s a vibe for sure - and that vibe is quiet optimism in spite of a broken world. It’s a bit like Forrest Gump without the ironic historical moments or really much of anything external going on. I wish more people could appreciate it.
2. On the wife being dead: I think people are looking for a "twist" in a movie that’s so clearly against that kind of contrivance. The movie thrives on the banalities of life. His wife’s bake sale should have gone wrong. I was expecting a big disappointment there. But no. The bus breaking down and people saying it could’ve exploded is also a comment on things that should’ve gone wrong but never did. Most people just talk about things going wrong that never do. Nothing monumental happens beyond the dog eating his book, which is the movie’s real explosion.

The twins stuff seems tied to Paterson thinking about his wife’s dream. It’s on his mind, so he notices them everywhere. It’s like when you buy a new car and suddenly you see the same one everywhere on the road. He’s just observing the meaningful things to him that week.

Also -- A-ha!
3. I love Patterson. I am a high school ELA teacher. One of my classes is the Creative Writing/Poetry elective at my school. I showed the class Patterson a couple of times. I agree with everything you said, but for me the theme I would put first is that it is about the creative process of an artist. Throughout the movie he writes, rewrites and starts again and we see it all on the screen. He is an artist like his wife. He drives a bus to sustain himself as an artist, so while he has a different temperament than his wife, he understands her as he pursues his own creative processes.
4. Nicely written analysis of the film.

There is no definitive answer to who this character is which is why I find the film fantastic. I love how Driver plays the character right on the edge. We don't know if he's the reserved, introspective, empathetic artist or the quiet, confused, trauma-laden psychopath.  
  
At the center is his poetry and that is also lined with complexity. Is it a tool for creativity or a tool to manage and mask pain? As you pointed out, has his military background inflicted so much emotional pain on him that being deliberate and focused to the point of compulsion is his way of controlling that angst bubbling inside of him?

We get hints here and there through long takes on his emotionless face. The slight movement of his mouth or eyes can reveal indignation or appreciation. Only once do we actually here him say that he dislikes something (the dog).

I think his wife mirrored one aspect of the artist persona and the tourist at the finale represents the other persona. The scene with the tourist, to me, was showing Driver's character what the next step in his evolution could be. Art for the sake of the individual, is not art until others can experience it. At this point, he isn't taking the road his wife drives on, but there is another road available.

Anyways, in my opinion there actually so much one can break down about this film because so much of the film, or rather all of it, isn't being told, but shown. It's easily one of the top 5 to 10 films I've seen in the past 10 years.
5. One of my favorite films. It’s weirdly rewatchable considering the light plot. It reminds me of Days of Heaven a bit in that I have a smile on my face through most of the movie at the quirky observations of Linda Manz’s character. It’s similar to Paterson’s observations on the people around him.

## 2. The Smashing Machine: Generic Sport Film Parading as an Art Film

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Even though I would still recommend the movie, I was super disappointed considering the talent involved. Once the credits rolled, I couldn’t help but think if there was a disconnect between Safdie’s original vision and the finished product as I was perplexed at what motivated the director to explore such dry subject matter. It seemed to be marketed as a Raging Bull-esque film that was more focused on the psychology of the sport professional rather than the sport itself, and despite starting with some interesting character elements (an identity that is entirely dependent on winning), the character focus is completely abandoned in favour of more generic tropes. Ultimately, it seems a like a film that is confused with its identity, caught between the desire to elevate itself beyond the generic tropes of the sport genre, and unfortunately, resulting in the very thing its attempting to transcend.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I couldn't handle the voice-over narration, which sounded like a graphic arts major attempting to sound like an MMA event commentator.

I thought it was well acted, especially impressed by Ryan Bader's chops.  I'd like to see him do more.
2. It is largely a remake of the HBO documentary of the same name. Like many scenes are remade nearly shot-for-shot and line-for-line which surprised me. I enjoyed it well enough, but I'm still unsure what the point of it is since I think the doc is ultimately better. I guess Safdie just wanted more people to know about Kerr since he's an interesting guy. It's successful in that regard at least.

## 3. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (November 23, 2025)

**Score:** 12
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One Battle After Another (2025 PTA). 

I did think about writing a post about this film, but in all honestly it left me so cold I don't think I could think of enough things to say about it. It left me completely blank, failing to engage me on any level. I didn't understand it - I didn't understand what it was or even what it was trying to be. I understood the story, but not what the story was for. My sense of confusion was not the enjoyable kind that PTA evoked so brilliantly in Inherent Vice. This was just frustration, and more than that, boredom. 

Of the many negative things I could say, I'm going to go for one I rarely hear, and that is the performance of DiCaprio in the lead role. I know he's a popular actor, but hear me out - watch this film again and pay attention to what he's doing. DiCaprio plays the surtext in every scene. He plays what's on the page in the most superficial way. He completely fails to bring life to his character. He fails to tell the story of his character - he fails to structure a performance that tells any story at all. I did not understand his character in this film, either in terms of his journey from start to finish or in the reality of his experience moment to moment. He gives a very straight forward, one note, on the nose performance in this film which is nowhere near enough to ground the viewer amid the chaos of the narrative. If I'm not pleading my case well here, I'd ask you to look at Phoenix in Inherent Vice for an example of how to be the emotional anchor in a sea of chaos, and how to tell the story of your character moment to moment.
2. Since last week: 

Running Man - Enjoyed it, better world building than OBAA, let down by its ending but overall a fun time 

Now You See Me: Now You Don't - Unexpected delight after I hated the first two. Doesn't entirely add up but a great time with a likable cast 

A Civil Action - Excellent script by Steven Zaillian - I didn't know about him before, but he had written some films I loved (Clear and Present Danger, Girl with the Dragon Tattoo, Moneyball). Outstanding performance from Travolta who I forgot was a genuinely fantastic presence with a fantastic foil in Robert Duvall. 

Rental Family - Liked it as a combination of Lost in Translation + Season 1 of The Rehearsal, though I think Brendan Frasier needs to be in more roles outside of his sad sack persona he's adopted between Condor Season 1 <> The Whale <> this

No Other Choice - Timely and darkly funny. Loved it, the reviews were on point. Likely #2 or #3 of my favorite movies of the year.
3. Dense week for me. Since last Saturday, here’s what I watched:

*The Alien Report* — Low-budget, FF schizo stuff with some surprisingly decent effects work.

*The Passion of Joan of Arc* — Got to see it in a theater with live orchestral accompaniment. Brilliant!

*American Movie* — Charming.

*Hamnet* — Believe what you’ve read about this one being a tear-jerker.

*Running Man* — Light romp

*Predator Badlands* — Marvelized *Predator* flick tackling masculinity.

*Zootopia* — One my partner picked in prep for the sequel. Mixed metaphor but you get the point.

*The Story of the Last Chrysanthemum* — Delicate and devastating.

*The Shop Around the Corner* — That Lubitsch touch. Suggests there’s more below the surface while keeping in check our own inflated egos.

*Angels Egg* — The surprise of the week, one I went to see on a whim based on a Reddit thread. Such a rich yarn, bringing together the post-apocalyptic and prehistoric, the Old Testament and the New, the historic and the present. 

*Keeper* — Perkins disappears up his own ass.

*His Girl Friday* — The fastest dialogue I’ve ever heard, and a scathing satire of newspapermen.

*Fantasmagorie* — Émile Cohl’s 1908 charmer still doing its thing in 2025.


*Rental Family* — Cute and life-affirming. A little rushed on some of the emotional beats, but it hits its mark.

*Sentimental Value* — It reminded me so much of *Persona* if instead of silence Elisabet chose to scream on stage. It’s a movie about a movie, which I’m a sucker for, but it’s really about relationships and generational trauma: the crack and either side of the crack daring the other to bridge it first. Top 3 of the year for sure.

*Pinocchio* — Enjoyed this one with some mind altering substances in the system. Probably my favorite original Disney film next to *Fantasia*.

*Baptism of Fire* — Nazi luftwaffe propaganda from the beginning of the war. Well made and rousing, its imagery of bombed out Poland is a haunting portent of the sheer devastation the war would bring and the two bombs that would end it.

*The Mortal Storm* — Something of a palette cleanser to the last film, very much akin to Malick’s *A Hidden Life* but starring *Shop Around the Corner* alumni James Stewart, Margaret Sullavan, and Frank Morgan as the Nazis come to power in Germany. Everyone speaking English with American accents now feels… prescient.
4. ​**Dear Ms.** (2025) \*\*\* Documentary about *Ms.* magazine done in three parts by three different directors. The first two parts recount the *Ms.* story well, but the third part feels like a different film with its focus on feminism's battle with pornography (that's surprisingly not all that interesting). Despite the unconventional approach, it's a pretty conventional doc. 

**Caddo Lake** (2024) \*\*\*\* After a slow start it becomes an effective little twisty thriller doing a high concept on a small budget, which is a great study for wannabe filmmakers out there. I certainly wasn't able to follow everything, but I felt assured the writer/directors had it all worked out, even if it doesn't ultimately add up to much. The actors do a fine job, but it's not helped by the annoying handheld camera throughout.

**Desert Hearts** (1985) \*\*\* Lesbian romance in a lovely Nevada setting that's clearly adapted from a novel because there's so much internalized emotions and underdeveloped secondary characters that you have to be clued into their mindset to respond deeply to it. Some kind of plot or subplot might have helped too, and they failed at the 1950s setting.

**Stranger Than Paradise** (1984) I thought Jim Jarmusch made movies about cool people, but the lead in this was a complete asshole. I didn't make it past 15 minutes. 

**Somebody Up There Likes Me** (1956) \*\*\*\* Scorsese clearly modeled *Raging Bull* on this biopic of Rocky Graziano. Paul Newman is convincing as a boxer, but he's not convincing as a dumb palooka. It's a showy, Brando-esque performance, but nobody knew that back then since this was Newman's breakout role, replacing the deceased James Dean. Age is an issue with a biopic that covers decades. Newman's too old to play the teen hoodlum, then they puff his face up with prosthetics to make him look older, and his mother is only six years older than him (a great performance by Eileen Heckart). Despite all that, it's an engaging and fast-paced drama in three distinct movements. Robert Wise directs the fight scenes very matter-of-factly until the final bout where he puts together a masterful montage. Look for Steve McQueen and (a very young) Veronica Cartwright in bit roles. The DVD offers a commentary by Robert Loggia, Paul Newman, Robert Wise, Peter Schickel, and Martin Scorsese, but despite the five speakers, there are still big stretches of silence. Scorsese offers very little, Schickel can barely get words out, and Newman merely pops in on a phone call for a couple of minutes.

**The Square Ring** (1953) \*\*\*\* Last week I watched a Basil Dearden soap opera set in an airport (*Out of the Clouds*) and this was the same thing but set in a boxing arena. This one worked great though because the setting is more confined, the climactic drama of the boxing bouts, and it has a delightfully snappy script with great humor and colorful characters adapted from a stageplay in classic Ealing studios style. It's a big ensemble cast, something Dearden does well, telling the story of six boxers. The focus is less on the fights (we don't even see the first one) and more on the behind-the-scenes operation of the arena, where we get to know the boxers and their drama in the locker room. 
5. Yesterday I watched Weapons and Badlands. 

Weapons had me for the first 2/3rds or so but as soon as the Aunt Gladys character enters the picture, the story loses all momentum, which is too bad because I specifically watched it to see Amy Madigan since she got nominated by AARP Movies for Grown-ups. Good for her, and she's good in the role, but this movie is just okay. 

Badlands -- wanted to see an early Sissy Spacek movie, heard a lot of good things about it on Awards Chatter podcast (which I'm learning not to trust because of course that guy is going to say good things about movies when he's talking to the people who are in them). I'm glad I watched this movie, but I never connected with it in the way I prefer to connect with movies. The characters were both so detached (probably the point but doesn't mean I enjoy it) and making such strange choices. I think I was supposed to probably feel something but I never did. Then I made the mistake of reading about the actual murderer (Starkweather) that the Kit Carruthers character (great name btw) was based on and that's living in my brain now. 

Between the two, I enjoyed Weapons more, probably because of the more modern story-telling, though neither movie is going to get more than three stars for me due to the complete lack of emotional resonance I felt about either.
6. [**WEEK # 255:**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)

"*Forgive...*"

Why did I wait so long before giving in to this philosophical exploration of quiet mourning, **THE TREE OF LIFE** (2011)? 

This is exactly my kind of a perfect collage of memories; evocative, melancholy, lyrical, deeply religious [I am deeply anti-religious, and still...] The loss of innocence in Waco, Texas in the 50's, the magic of suburban childhood told like it was never told before, the pain of losing a son... Even the lengthy 'Creation of the cosmos' parts were moving. Even the framing of grown-up Sean Penn pondering the death of his brother years ago... 

**10/10 - one of my best films of the year.** My 5th by Terrence Malick (and possibly my favorite of his so far).

🍿 

**NO OTHER CHOICE** (2025), my 4th, highly-praised drama by Park Chan-wook. This is a remake of the Costa-Gavras 2005 thriller 'The Axe', which was also based on a Donald E. Westlake novel. The premise was intriguing: A senior manager at a large paper company is laid off, and since he can't be hired again, he starts killing the few selected candidates who apply for the same high-level job that he does. 

It has 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes, but I was disappointed and bored with it. While the French dark political thriller was able to intelligently criticize both capitalism and the bourgeois, and still deliver a compelling story, this (long!) version was a light comedy that missed that or any other mark. 

🍿 

**SOMMARNATTENS LEENDE X 3:**

🍿 "*You old goat, you brute, you long-nosed camel!*"

Re-watch of the original Ingmar Bergman's **SMILES OF A SUMMER NIGHT** (1955). This frothy, delightful sex farce, another with 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes, is one that can be enjoyed from the first to last scene. Evocative of the old-thyme allure of midsummer nights in Scandinavia. Advokat Gunnar Björnstrand with his ridiculous goatee, clownish servant "Frid" (who repeated the same role a couple of years later as "Plog" in 'The Seventh Seal'), Jarl Kulle (who repeated his role as the elder officer in 'Babette's Feast'), Naima Wifstrand (who replayed herself as Isak's old mother in' Wild Strawberries'), and of course the 4 wonderful young women everybody is so crazy about. **10/10.** ♻️

🍿 “*Little Schnapps, I’m sure?… You never said ’No’ to Schnaps?…*”

So of course I had to watch **A LITTLE NIGHT MUSIC** (1977), my 8th Stephen Sondheim production. (Sondheim picked such odd collection of topics for his musicals; A Seurat painting for his 'Sunday in the park with George', Brothers Grimm fairy tales for 'Into the woods', a Shakespeare retelling in 'West Side Story', 'Sweeney Todd', the murdering barber...)

Here he chose a 20 yo Bergman comedy. Elizabeth Taylor (who couldn't sing) was Desiree Armfeldt, and sexy Diana Rigg was her rival. There were two memorable numbers in it, 'Weekend in the country' and 'Send in the clowns'. 

🍿 "*I'll show you what Dulcy calls A Mexican Cartwheel...*"

And more: **A MIDSUMMER NIGHT'S SEX COMEDY** (1982) was basically an American remake of Bergman's period classic. Like most remakes of foreign films, it's not as good as the original, even though Woody Allen would have liked you to think that it was. However, it's not as horrible as some of his other comedies. In it, not only he, but the all 6 horny members of this comedy are always looking to get laid. Again I have to ask: How could people, during his first 20 years, think that Woody Allen was "sexy" in any way?

The good: Music score by Mendelssohn, and cinematography by Gordon Willis (Including beautiful shots of nature). Re-watch ♻️.

🍿 

**THE ENTERTAINER**, an early Tony Richardson feature (1960), starring Laurence Olivier as a third-rate music-hall performer in a provincial seaside resort town, who is sinking into despair, defeat and nihilism. This was the film debut of both Alan Bates and Albert Finney, as well as an early performance of Joan Plowright.

🍿 

**2 DOCUMENTARIES WITH LYNN SHELTON:**

🍿 **HALF THE PICTURE** (2018), another (sad!) documentary about discrimination against women directors in Hollywood.

In recent years, I've come to consider the systemic sexism and misogyny which is baked into our lifestyles and politics, as one of the main reasons why everything’s gone to shit. The topic of The Patriarchy infuriates me, and I hate the entitlement of White Male Supremacy more and more.

This documentary is mostly about Hollywood "employment practices", because "international" cinema is more inclusive. But the American entertainment establishment is frustratingly anti-women, and is not getting any better. Incremental progress doesn't work.

The film is structured around home-made conversations with about 30 female directors. *I have made a point during my film project to watch more films directed by women (152 so far in 2025)*. [*Female Director*]

🍿 And since I never listened to any "podcasts", I only heard of grumpy comedian Marc Maron when he interviewed Obama in his LA garage. Recently I've seen some of his stand ups and I liked them. But unlike f. ex. George Carlin, there's a principle of diminished returns with him. His newest over-share **ARE WE GOOD?** (2025) is dealing with his grief over the death of his partner Lynn Shelton, and it seems that their relationship was the best element in his narcissistic life. I had to drink two large G&T just to finish this superficial drivel. 2/10. 

🍿 

**BUSTER KEATON X 3:**

🍿 **THE BALLOONATIC**, one of his last 2-reelers from 1923, where he finds himself on top of a hot-air balloon, and later in the outdoors, battling a bear and rapids of a river.

🍿 **THE GREAT BUSTER: A CELEBRATION** was a Peter Bogdanovich biography from 2018. I forgot that Keaton started his vaudeville career at the age of 11 months, and that just before he died, he received the longest standing ovation at the Venice Film Festival, a moving, late acknowledgement for his lasting contribution to the art of cinema. 

But whatever goodwill this serviceable biopic had, it squandered it all by using a long list of annoying talking heads who kept mouthing inane platitudes about his genius, which soured the whole experience for me. 5/10.  

🍿 "*A boat named 'Damfino'…*"

At least that documentary prompted me to watch **COLLEGE**, one of his last independent features (1927). Here he plays "Ronald", a bookish scholar, who must get in "fisical" shape in order to win the love of the girl. It includes a good scene of him as a soda-jerk, but also one where he plays a 'colored' waiter in black-face.
His rival, the "bully", locks himself up with the girl in her dorm room, so that she will be expelled when discovered, and "will have to marry him" (?!?...)

*(Continued below)*
  - *(Continued)*

**2 FRANKENSTEIN’S:**

🍿 I joined a new film club here in Copenhagen, and I ventured out into the cold night to see a movie on a big screen for a change - only my 2nd time to do so in 6 years. 

**50 (!)** years ago, I saw **YOUNG FRANKENSTEIN** for the first time in a similar cinema, but I never realized how unfunny it was.  Its delayed delivery style is no longer clever. The only scene that retained its "classic" status was the 'Puttin' on the Ritz' dance. And - the incredible sexism (and rape joke!) aged badly. 3/10. Re-watch ♻️.

🍿 **FRANKENSTEIN**, the latest version from 2025, and my 3rd utterly-uninteresting, big budget fantasy by Guillermo del Toro (after ‘The shape of water’ and Nightmare Alley). Stylized bullshit and childish romanticism made this a loooong, boring exercise in excess. It was nice that he added a Danish framing subplot though. **2/10.** 

🍿 

**THE SHORTS:**

🍿 **THE CASE OF THE ELEVATOR DUCK** (1974), a wholesome educational short by Joan Micklin Silver, made just before 'Hester Street'. A black kid lives on the 16th floor of a tenant apartment building, and while playing detective, he finds a duck in the elevator. [*Female Director*].

🍿 **GLASS** (1958), the first Oscar win for The Netherlands, a documentary about artisan glassblowers. [A highly-satisfying jazzy poem with terrific score, performed by The Pim Jacobs Quintet.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=d3QEpQ9ozVU) **10/10.** (*This is the exact review I wrote when I saw it the first time!*) Re-watch ♻️

🍿 "*Life is but a bowl of Chitlins...*"

In **BOWL OF PORK** (1998) Dave Chappelle as Forrest Gump tells a black couple on the bench how he ended up instigating the Rodney King riots... [*Female Director*].

🍿 **THE FINAL DAYS** (2000). Before 'Somebody feed Phil', Philip Rosenthal directed a "comedy" sketch with Bill Clinton, as he's about to leave the White House. With a cameo of Kevin Spacey, because of course...

🍿 **RESIGNATION** (2016), an unexplained Sci-fi short from Texas about a man driving to an enigmatic meeting before the end of the world. 2/10.

🍿 Just [another frequent re-watching](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6up9YgF7Ocs) of Hungarian animator Réka Bucsi's **SYMPHONY NO. 42** (2014), because why not? ♻️. [*Female Director*].

🍿 *"Nature wants to regenerate...*" Cinematographer Emmanuel Lubezki's own short **THE KNOWING** (2024), about "Nature", and an underwater meeting with a mother whale.

[**(ALL MY FILM REVIEWS - HERE).**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)
7. **Eddington** directed by Ari Aster (USA/2025) - This film is set in a small town in New Mexico during the summer of 2020, when covid and BLM protests were on the news every night.  The town’s sheriff is Joe Cross (Joaquin Phoenix) and he is a decent, if not terribly bright, man.  The town’s mayor, Ted Garcia (Pedro Pascal) is up for re-election soon and is *not* a nice guy, but he takes his duties to his town seriously: big picture he is wooing a tech company to build a data center nearby, short term his obligation is to enforce the governor’s covid mask mandate.  The sheriff (an asthmatic) won’t wear a mask and thinks the risk is exaggerated.  These men have disliked each other for a long time and this trivial disagreement sparks the chaos which will soon engulf the small desert town. Baffled and frustrated by life in general, Joe Cross impulsively decides to run for mayor himself and promptly turns his county office into a campaign headquarters and appoints his few deputies to be his campaign staff.  All this might seem to promise the viewer a sharp satire or gentle comedy about the lunacy of American politics and/or the bottomless gullibility of the unwashed masses.  Imagine what Billy Wilder or Bill Forsyth or perhaps even Robert Altman might have made from this set-up.  Unfortunately, the next two hours of viewing this movie will make it clear that Mr. Aster should never be mentioned in the same paragraph as those filmmakers.

It all leads to violence, although on one’s first viewing the plot is rather difficult to follow, since the actions of several minor characters propel events forward.  The sheriff’s wife (a waste of Emma Stone) and mother-in-law play a key role in the growing animosity as well.  The good news is that fine performances and some good, funny lines make this film watchable.  The bad news is that things just get more implausible as we near the conclusion of a film that feels longer than it needs to be.
8. **Batman Begins (2005, Christopher Nolan) — re-watch** — In a way, I think it’s almost unfortunate that *The Dark Knight* ended up being one of the greatest movies ever made, because having it to compare to has allowed a lot of people — including myself up until this re-watch — to overlook just how great its predecessor is in its own right. The Nolan Batman movies have often been described as being darker and/or more philosophical than most superhero movies, and both of those are certainly true; re-watching this, I was struck by how much more *vicious* Nolan makes Batman than I had remembered, and how much the question of what really separates him from a villain like Ra’s feels like a genuinely profound question, not just something set up to be answered didactically or hand waved away. Yet, one thing that tends to get lost in these discussions is that, just as pure action/thrillers, they’re also some of the most *fun* in the genre’s history. Watching scenes like the moment Batman revs up the Batmobile for the first time gave me a kind of visceral rush that I haven’t had watching a movie in quite a while. A must-watch. **9/10**

**The Wolf of Wall Street (2013, Martin Scorsese) — re-watch** — Full disclosure: I didn’t quite make it to the end of re-watching this one. I got tired of it and bailed before getting to the last 40 minutes.

After realizing on rewatch that *The Departed* was far better than I’d remembered it being, I thought it might be worth checking out another of Scorsese’s more recent hits, to see whether maybe I’d been overly harsh on his work in the 2010s in general. Sadly, that doesn’t seem to be the case; *The Wolf of Wall Street* still seems to me to be just as amateurish and tedious as it was the first time I watched it. I know the “media-literate” take on the movie is supposed to be that it’s a brutal takedown of capitalism and that anyone who thinks it’s celebrating the wealthy is an idiot, but, frankly, if Martin Scorsese really thought people’s main takeaway from watching Jordan Belfort fuck Margot Robbie on top of a huge pile of money — that’s not hyperbole, that exact thing literally happens in the movie — would be a reflection on the evils of wealth inequality or something, that’s not a case of the *viewers* being idiots. It’s not like the movie particularly goes out of its way to highlight the harm that Belfort’s crimes caused, or even to suggest that Belfort himself was particularly unsatisfied with what he got out of it; apart from some scenes in the last third, basically all the movie does is make being a scammer seem like the coolest thing ever.

But even putting aside any issues one might have with the message it conveys, and just looking at it as a movie, *Wolf* is just not very well-made. The script is one of the most bloated in Scorsese’s filmography, and, stylistically, it’s largely just a retread of ideas he’d already used to much better effect in *Goodfellas* and *Casino*. By far the best thing about the movie is Leonardo DiCaprio, who’s so magnetic as Belfort that he almost single-handedly keeps the movie at least tolerable, but it’s just not enough reason to justify sitting through the entire thing. Do not recommend. **4/10**

**Movie of the week:** *Batman Begins*
9. I have been trying to go to the theaters every week, so I have been watching all the new releases.


- Sentimental Value: every scene a lot of beautiful human emotions to unpack. Left me crying in the theater while the credits were rolling. Probably my favorite movie of the year
- Rental Family: I expected a feel-good story, and it is a feel good story but every cliche amped up to the max to the point of being cringe. I could swear whoever made the lines for the girl was using ChatGPT.

## 4. What was the purpose of that scene in Sentimental Value?

**Score:** 1
**Body:** I got out of Sentimental Value last night and I thought it was excellent. Renate, Stellan, Elle, and Inga all gave incredible performances and I hope to see some Oscar wins for them. However, I left the theater a bit confused about the narrative purpose of a particular scene. In the third act, Gustav collapses or goes into cardiac arrest--I don't remember what specifically happened to him, but he was hospitalized. I was expecting it to have a huge impact on Nora or Agnes, but in the very next scene, Gustav ends up fine and it kind of seemed like there were no consequences or stakes. Can someone smarter than me explain why this was included?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He wasn't "fine" in the next scene, he was recovering. Then we jump ahead to many months later and he's retooled his film and put the financing back together.

It simply represents his bottom. It's the catalyst for Nora finally deciding to read the script, and a nominal turning point for both of them

## 5. Why do movie trailers nowadays show a preview before they begin?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Imagine if the first trailer for Jurassic Park showed all the dinosaur scenes in a 5 second preview before the trailer started. Why do modern movie trailers do this? Trailers have always been the best format to advertise and tease a film, yet this new technique basically depletes so much of the tension building that old trailers had. Is this trend due to mobile devices or something? I find it so appalling, that I won't even watch trailers anymore. Hollywood just keeps finding a way to sink deeper I guess.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. this is only on youtube and other social media platforms. it's so that your attention is grabbed immediately while you're scrolling past the video.

i'm surprised you're complaining about this now, though, as it's been a common trick for like fifteen years or more now. 

that being said, these previews of the trailers don't play when you watch them in a movie theatre.
  - And it’s not *just* because of attention spans, it’s specifically because YouTube makes ads unskippable for the first 5-15 secs, and so trailers these days are built to be turned into pre-roll ads, and therefore put all the most exciting bits in the first few secs to entice people not to skip once the “skip” button appears.
2. Its specifically because these trailers are shown as ads. You go to watch a different video, and the beginning of the trailer is shown as an ad. After a few seconds you can skip the ad, so their goal is to get you interested to not skip it.
3. It's due to the viewing habits, ad dismissal time-out, and algorithms on social media. 

If you see a trailer as an ad on YouTube, you can dismiss it after 5-15 seconds, meaning that those initial few seconds need to sell the entire movie. 

The same is true when ads are delivered on other socials like Instagram, FB, or Tiktok. People scroll fast, and so trailers hook viewers more effectively if they're designed in this way. It's all based on data that shows the very real effectiveness of this editing practice. 

It's been common for at least 10 years, so it's definitely not new.

It's not really a Hollywood problem, it's just a byproduct of digital platforms. I think theatres typically get different cuts of trailers without the pre-trailer stinger.
4. I feel you. The other day* my friend and I went to see a movie in the theater. During the coming attractions we remarked that some previews made us feel like we had "watched the whole movie."

* in 2006
  - This is why I generally avoid trailers unless I'm physically in the theater. They tend to spoil a lot of the big moments of the film without really changing my decision to watch the movie beyond just reading the synopsis on IMDB.
5. Because people’s attention spans are short and everything online is competing with everything else.

It’s the trailer’s way of showing people this is worth their next five minutes.

Minimum character requirements are kind of a drag sometimes. I hope this meets it now.
